nativeRange.habitat: Passes and outer reef slopes at depths of 1–30 m; typically associated with rocky rubble and coral reefs

diet.notes: Tends to accept a wide range of aquarium foods including vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, bloodworms, glassworms, as well as flake and pellet foods. In the wild has been documented feeding on algae and small zooplankton. Generally advised to feed 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: gobies and blennies of similar size
avoidWith: other small damsels unless in large tanks with ample territories

summary: The Canary Deep Water Damsel is a vivid yellow damselfish found on the outer reefs and reef slopes of the Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and surrounding Pacific island groups. It is generally considered easy to keep and reef-compatible, though it tends to be semi-aggressive toward similar-sized fish, particularly in smaller aquariums.
careNotes: The Canary Deep Water Damsel inhabits passes and outer reef slopes across the Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and the Pitcairn Islands, typically found at depths between 1 and 30 metres among rocky rubble and coral reef structure. Despite carrying a 'deep water' common name, it adapts well to standard reef aquarium conditions. A tank of at least 200 litres is generally advised to give this territorial fish adequate room, with plenty of live rock and open crevices for shelter. Like other damselfish, it tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward similar-sized or smaller fish, so tankmates should be chosen accordingly. Water parameters should fall within standard reef ranges: specific gravity 1.020–1.025, temperature 22.5–27.5°C, and pH 8.1–8.4.
breedingNotes: The Canary Deep Water Damsel is oviparous and forms distinct pairs during spawning. Males have been documented guarding and aerating demersal egg clutches — eggs attached to solid substrate — following the typical Pomacentridae breeding pattern. Captive breeding reports are not available for this species.
